prompt: Translate the following English sentence into French, Japanese, and Arabic, and add a brief note on any phrase that was tricky to render faithfully: "The silence between us said more than any argument could have."
response:
**French:**  
Le silence entre nous en disait plus que n’importe quel argument n’aurait pu le faire.

**Japanese:**  
私たちの間の沈黙は、どんな議論よりも多くを語っていた。

**Arabic:**  
قال الصمت بيننا أكثر مما كان بإمكان أي جدال أن يقوله.

---

**Note on tricky phrasing:**  
The phrase “said more than any argument could have” involves a comparative construction with an implicit counterfactual (i.e., even the best possible argument wouldn’t match the silence). In French, “n’aurait pu le faire” captures this hypothetical nuance well. Japanese uses “どんな議論よりも” (“more than any argument”) and the verb “語っていた” (“was speaking/telling”), which naturally conveys the eloquence of silence. Arabic uses “كان بإمكان... أن” to express the conditional capability (“could have”), though the phrasing is slightly more compact; the verb “قال” (“said”) is used metaphorically for silence, which is idiomatic in Arabic as in English. All three languages handle the metaphor well, but subtle shifts in verb tense or modality were needed to maintain naturalness.
